+{"id":"bv-3sm","content_hash":"4e9b28302a430ea5290016b9b2abc745770c4eda934f00865ef8f241064a0fd9","title":"Add pkg/loader error handling and edge case tests","description":"pkg/loader/loader.go has only 874 bytes of tests for 3.6KB of production code.
Current coverage: 63.2% - needs improvement to 90%+.

## Current Gaps
- Error handling paths not tested
- Edge cases not covered
- File I/O errors not simulated

## Required Tests (NO MOCKS - use real files)

### File System Error Tests
- [ ] Non-existent .beads directory
- [ ] Non-existent beads.jsonl file
- [ ] Permission denied on file read
- [ ] Empty beads.jsonl file (0 bytes)
- [ ] File with only whitespace/newlines

### JSONL Parsing Tests
- [ ] Valid single-line JSON
- [ ] Valid multi-line JSONL
- [ ] Malformed JSON (missing brace, invalid syntax)
- [ ] Valid JSON but invalid Issue schema
- [ ] Mixed valid/invalid lines (partial load)
- [ ] Very long lines (>1MB single issue)
- [ ] Unicode in titles/descriptions (emoji, CJK, RTL)

### Path Resolution Tests
- [ ] Relative path resolution
- [ ] Absolute path handling
- [ ] Symlink handling
- [ ] Path with spaces
- [ ] Path with special characters

### Large File Tests
- [ ] 1000+ issues loading time
- [ ] 10000+ issues memory usage
- [ ] Streaming vs buffered comparison

## Test Implementation Strategy
Use t.TempDir() for isolated test directories:
```go
func TestLoadIssues_EmptyFile(t *testing.T) {
    dir := t.TempDir()
    beadsDir := filepath.Join(dir, ".beads")
    os.MkdirAll(beadsDir, 0755)
    os.WriteFile(filepath.Join(beadsDir, "beads.jsonl"), []byte{}, 0644)
    
    issues, err := LoadIssues(dir)
    // Assert empty slice, no error
}
```

 {"id":"bv-52t","content_hash":"0050b85277b55f9b49ea47ccfa20e527f0ba901549899af01ae15ade26f81520","title":"Impact Radar & Drift Alerts","description":"Detect significant changes in graph metrics and alert when structure degrades.

## Background & Motivation
Graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, cycles) can degrade over time without anyone noticing. A new cycle might be introduced, or a bottleneck might emerge. Proactive detection prevents issues from compounding.

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: Early warning when project health degrades
- For AI Agents: Validate changes before committing - 'did I introduce a cycle?'

## Technical Approach
1. Baseline storage (snapshot of metrics at a point in time)
2. Drift calculator (compare current to baseline)
3. Threshold configuration (when to alert)
4. CLI: --check-drift for CI integration

## Alert Types
- New cycles detected (critical)
- Significant PageRank changes (potential new bottleneck)
- Betweenness spike (new critical path node)
- Density increase (complexity growing)

## Success Criteria
- CI can run --check-drift and fail on regressions
- Clear exit codes: 0=OK, 1=critical, 2=warning
- Human-readable and JSON output available","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-11-26T23:37:14.250670509Z","updated_at":"2025-11-30T15:05:08.015767175Z","closed_at":"2025-11-30T15:05:08.015767175Z","source_repo":".","labels":["ci","monitoring","quality"]}

+{"id":"bv-66u","content_hash":"40d2e43f6ff41bd01cf1443f0659abea827dc5e7e25edd1a8c43a38acfcf126d","title":"Add pkg/ui/delegate.go rendering tests","description":"pkg/ui/delegate.go has NO test coverage despite containing complex list item rendering logic.

## Current Code (7.6KB)
- IssueDelegate struct with 4 methods
- Complex Render() method with width calculations
- Priority badge rendering
- Status badge rendering
- Diff status badges
- Repo prefix badges (workspace mode)
- Selection highlighting

## Required Tests (NO MOCKS)

### IssueDelegate Interface Tests
- [ ] Height() returns consistent value (1)
- [ ] Spacing() returns consistent value (0)
- [ ] Update() returns nil (no-op)

### Render() Output Tests
Create tests that capture rendered output:
- [ ] Basic issue rendering (ID, title, status visible)
- [ ] Selected vs unselected styling differs
- [ ] Priority badge shows correct P0-P5 format
- [ ] Status badge shows correct status text
- [ ] Very long title truncated correctly
- [ ] Very short title padded correctly
- [ ] Unicode in title handled correctly

### Width Calculation Tests
- [ ] Narrow width (40 chars) - minimal info shown
- [ ] Medium width (80 chars) - standard layout
- [ ] Wide width (120 chars) - all columns visible
- [ ] Very wide (200 chars) - no overflow

### Workspace Mode Tests
- [ ] WorkspaceMode=false -> no repo badge
- [ ] WorkspaceMode=true -> repo badge appears
- [ ] Repo badge color consistent for same prefix
- [ ] Repo badge truncated if prefix > 4 chars

### Priority Hints Tests
- [ ] ShowPriorityHints=false -> no hint indicator
- [ ] ShowPriorityHints=true with increase hint -> ↑ shown
- [ ] ShowPriorityHints=true with decrease hint -> ↓ shown
- [ ] Issue without hint in map -> no indicator

### Diff Status Tests
- [ ] DiffStatusNone -> no badge
- [ ] DiffStatusNew -> 🆕 badge
- [ ] DiffStatusClosed -> ✅ badge
- [ ] DiffStatusModified -> ~ badge

## Test Strategy
Use a bytes.Buffer to capture Render() output:
```go
func TestIssueDelegate_Render(t *testing.T) {
    delegate := IssueDelegate{Theme: DefaultTheme()}
    item := IssueItem{Issue: model.Issue{ID: "TEST-1", Title: "Test Issue"}}
    
    // Create mock list.Model with enough context
    items := []list.Item{item}
    l := list.New(items, delegate, 80, 10)
    
    var buf bytes.Buffer
    delegate.Render(&buf, l, 0, item)
    
    output := buf.String()
    if !strings.Contains(output, "TEST-1") {
        t.Error("ID not in output")
    }
}
```

+{"id":"bv-6g7","content_hash":"9fd1aa38c4cd0f29a27bd22663a3d19e996cbdc39f951dc3412705805700a1fa","title":"Refactor and test pkg/ui/model.go Update() method","description":"model.go is 71KB with 39+ functions but only 6 unit tests. The Update() method alone is 500+ lines handling all keyboard events. Current coverage: 52.5%.

## Problem
The Update() method is a massive switch statement that:
- Handles 7+ different view modes
- Contains 200+ key bindings
- Manages complex state transitions
- Is nearly impossible to unit test in current form

## Refactoring Strategy

### Phase 1: Extract Key Handlers (NO behavior change)
Extract each case in Update() into a named method:
```go
// Before (in Update):
case key.Matches(msg, keys.Enter):
    // 50 lines of logic

// After:
case key.Matches(msg, keys.Enter):
    return m.handleEnterKey(msg)

func (m Model) handleEnterKey(msg tea.KeyMsg) (Model, tea.Cmd) {
    // Same 50 lines, now testable
}
```

### Phase 2: Add Unit Tests for Each Handler
- [ ] handleListKeys() - all list navigation keys
- [ ] handleBoardKeys() - board view navigation
- [ ] handleGraphKeys() - graph view navigation
- [ ] handleActionableKeys() - actionable view
- [ ] handleRecipePickerKeys() - recipe selection
- [ ] handleInsightsKeys() - insights navigation
- [ ] handleTimeTravelInputKeys() - time travel input
- [ ] handleQuitConfirmKeys() - quit confirmation

### Phase 3: Test State Transitions
- [ ] List -> Detail view transition
- [ ] List -> Board view transition
- [ ] List -> Graph view transition
- [ ] Detail -> back to List
- [ ] Time travel mode entry/exit
- [ ] Filter mode entry/exit
- [ ] Search mode entry/exit

### Phase 4: Test View Rendering
Extract and test render functions:
- [ ] renderListWithHeader()
- [ ] renderSplitView()
- [ ] renderFooter()
- [ ] renderQuitConfirm()
- [ ] renderTimeTravelInput()

## Required Tests (39 functions need tests)

### Core State Tests
- [ ] NewModel() initializes all fields correctly
- [ ] Init() returns expected command
- [ ] View() produces valid output for each view mode

### Message Handling Tests
- [ ] WindowSizeMsg updates dimensions
- [ ] IssuesUpdatedMsg refreshes list
- [ ] VersionCheckMsg shows update notification
- [ ] AnalysisCompleteMsg updates insights

### Filter Tests
- [ ] applyFilter() with empty filter
- [ ] applyFilter() with matching text
- [ ] applyFilter() with no matches
- [ ] Filter clears when pressing Escape

### Recipe Tests
- [ ] applyRecipe() applies sort
- [ ] applyRecipe() applies filter
- [ ] Recipe picker shows available recipes

### Time Travel Tests
- [ ] enterTimeTravelMode() sets up diff state
- [ ] exitTimeTravelMode() restores normal state
- [ ] Time travel input parses dates correctly
- [ ] Invalid time travel input shows error

## Files
- pkg/ui/model.go (refactor)
- pkg/ui/model_test.go (enhance significantly)
- pkg/ui/handlers.go (new file for extracted handlers)
- pkg/ui/handlers_test.go (new test file)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] model.go Update() is under 100 lines
- [ ] Each key handler has ≥3 test cases
- [ ] Coverage increases from 52.5% to ≥80%
- [ ] No behavior changes (refactor only)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-30T15:33:54.059160664Z","updated_at":"2025-11-30T15:33:54.059160664Z","source_repo":".","labels":["refactoring","testing","ui"]}
 {"id":"bv-81q","content_hash":"4165b8fe5ab42625b6bb6d24759225f3ad351aa8f955106d4bb8879217cf7f27","title":"Add pkg/model/types.go validation tests","description":"pkg/model/types.go defines core data structures but has ZERO test coverage.

## Example Test Structure

func TestIssue_Validation(t *testing.T) {

        {"empty ID", Issue{ID: "", Title: "Test"}, true},
        {"empty title", Issue{ID: "TEST-1", Title: ""}, true},
    }
    // ... test implementation
}

 {"id":"bv-cam.10","content_hash":"645ab041e0ffd3f5707c5de4904405428e91e643eda4ba65dda0a328a9ea2a14","title":"E2E tests: drift config customization","description":"E2E tests for custom drift thresholds via .bv/drift.yaml:

## Custom Threshold Tests
1. TestE2EConfigHigherThresholds:
   - Set density_warning_pct: 200
   - Trigger 100% density increase
   - Verify: NO warning (below new threshold)

2. TestE2EConfigLowerThresholds:
   - Set blocked_increase_threshold: 1
   - Add just 2 blocked issues
   - Verify: warning triggered (above threshold)

3. TestE2EConfigPartialOverride:
   - Set only density_warning_pct: 75
   - Verify other values use defaults
   - Trigger 50% density (should be info, not warning)

## Config File Creation
4. TestE2EConfigFileCreation:
   - Verify drift.yaml path is .bv/drift.yaml
   - Verify YAML syntax is valid
   - Verify comments are preserved

## Logging
- Log config file contents
- Log which thresholds are active
- Log expected vs actual behavior","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-30T15:28:04.573507685Z","updated_at":"2025-11-30T15:28:04.573507685Z","source_repo":".","labels":["drift","e2e","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bv-cam.10","depends_on_id":"bv-cam.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-11-30T15:28:04.585194267Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bv-lkk","content_hash":"0b85b6455a2d66914ad1621602f0cf1c3b5f88321dff750b40966024282eb8d2","title":"AI-Aware Kanban Autoprioritizer","description":"Use graph metrics (PageRank, betweenness, freshness, blocker count) to suggest priority adjustments and surface high-impact items.

## Background & Motivation
The analysis package computes rich metrics but they're only visible in the insights panel. Users and agents want actionable recommendations: 'These 5 items should be prioritized because they unblock the most work.'

## Value Proposition
- For Humans: 'Smart suggestions' that explain why items should be reprioritized
- For AI Agents: --robot-priority provides a ranked work queue with reasoning

## Technical Approach
1. Create priority scoring function combining multiple metrics
2. Generate recommendations comparing current vs suggested priority
3. CLI: --robot-priority outputs priority-ranked JSON with explanations
4. TUI: 'Suggested' badge/indicator on items

## Scoring Function
impact_score = (
    0.3 * normalized_pagerank +
    0.3 * normalized_betweenness +
    0.2 * blocker_count / max_blocker_count +
    0.1 * freshness_penalty +
    0.1 * explicit_priority_boost
)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-11-26T23:36:10.969534571Z","updated_at":"2025-11-27T01:03:35.76590347Z","closed_at":"2025-11-27T01:03:35.76590347Z","source_repo":".","labels":["ai-agent","analysis","prioritization"]}
 {"id":"bv-lkk.1","content_hash":"360ba65418957ce86f64c6388f5f4c07d43c8bd1ed5e64c506eaec4702b2583c","title":"Implement priority scoring function","description":"Create composite scoring combining PageRank (30%), Betweenness (30%), Blocker count (20%), Staleness (10%), Explicit priority (10%).

## Algorithm
func (a *Analyzer) ComputeImpactScore(issue *model.Issue) ImpactScore:
  - Normalize all metrics to 0-1 range
  - Apply weights: 0.3*pagerank + 0.3*betweenness + 0.2*blockerRatio + 0.1*staleness + 0.1*priorityBoost
  - Return: IssueID, ImpactScore, ScoreBreakdown map

## Staleness Penalty
- Days since update, normalized: min(daysSinceUpdate / 30, 1.0)
- Older items get slight penalty to surface them

## Priority Boost
- P0=1.0, P1=0.75, P2=0.5, P3=0.25, P4=0.0 (higher priority = higher boost)

- pkg/analysis/priority.go: New scoring logic
- pkg/analysis/priority_test.go: Tests with known values","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-26T23:39:56.597660662Z","updated_at":"2025-11-27T00:26:30.580840974Z","closed_at":"2025-11-27T00:26:30.580840974Z","source_repo":".","labels":["analysis","core-logic"]}

## Output Structure
type PriorityRecommendation struct {
    IssueID           string
    CurrentPriority   int
    SuggestedPriority int
    ImpactScore       float64
    Confidence        float64  // 0-1, higher when score difference is large
    Reasoning         []string // Human-readable explanations
}

## Reasoning Templates
- 'Blocks N high-priority items' (when UnblocksCount > 2)
- 'Critical path bottleneck' (when Betweenness > 0.5)
- 'High centrality in graph' (when PageRank > 0.3)
- 'Stale for N days' (when staleness penalty applied)

## Confidence Calculation
- Large score vs priority mismatch = high confidence
- Multiple reasoning factors = higher confidence
- Single weak signal = low confidence

+{"id":"bv-oxm","content_hash":"67afd1b77ded151e0de527daace28a4a3f3a503d2b0c55a3392bd3b00ba25977","title":"Add detailed test logging infrastructure","description":"Tests currently have minimal logging, making failures hard to diagnose. Need structured, detailed logging throughout.

## Current Problems
- Test failures show minimal context
- No timing information in test output
- Hard to trace which step failed in complex tests
- No structured format for CI parsing

## Logging Infrastructure Needed

### Test Logger Package
Create pkg/testutil/logger.go:
```go
package testutil

type TestLogger struct {
    t       *testing.T
    name    string
    started time.Time
}

func NewLogger(t *testing.T) *TestLogger {
    return &TestLogger{t: t, name: t.Name(), started: time.Now()}
}

func (l *TestLogger) Step(format string, args ...interface{}) {
    elapsed := time.Since(l.started)
    l.t.Logf("[%s +%v] %s", l.name, elapsed, fmt.Sprintf(format, args...))
}

func (l *TestLogger) Input(name string, value interface{}) {
    l.t.Logf("[%s] INPUT %s = %+v", l.name, name, value)
}

func (l *TestLogger) Output(name string, value interface{}) {
    l.t.Logf("[%s] OUTPUT %s = %+v", l.name, name, value)
}

func (l *TestLogger) Error(err error) {
    l.t.Logf("[%s] ERROR: %v", l.name, err)
}

func (l *TestLogger) Duration() time.Duration {
    return time.Since(l.started)
}
```

### Assertion Helpers with Logging
```go
func AssertEqual(t *testing.T, log *TestLogger, name string, got, want interface{}) {
    if got != want {
        log.Error(fmt.Errorf("%s: got %v, want %v", name, got, want))
        t.Errorf("%s: got %v, want %v", name, got, want)
    } else {
        log.Step("%s = %v (OK)", name, got)
    }
}

func AssertNoError(t *testing.T, log *TestLogger, op string, err error) {
    if err != nil {
        log.Error(fmt.Errorf("%s failed: %v", op, err))
        t.Fatalf("%s failed: %v", op, err)
    }
    log.Step("%s succeeded", op)
}
```

### Usage Example
```go
func TestLoadIssues_LargeFile(t *testing.T) {
    log := testutil.NewLogger(t)
    
    log.Step("Creating test directory")
    dir := t.TempDir()
    
    log.Step("Generating %d test issues", 1000)
    issues := generateIssues(1000)
    log.Input("issue_count", len(issues))
    
    log.Step("Writing JSONL file")
    writeJSONL(dir, issues)
    
    log.Step("Loading issues from file")
    loaded, err := LoadIssues(dir)
    testutil.AssertNoError(t, log, "LoadIssues", err)
    
    log.Output("loaded_count", len(loaded))
    log.Step("Test completed in %v", log.Duration())
}
```

### CI-Friendly Output Format
Add structured output mode for CI:
```go
// When TEST_LOG_FORMAT=json
{"test":"TestLoadIssues","step":"loading","elapsed_ms":42,"status":"ok"}
{"test":"TestLoadIssues","step":"complete","elapsed_ms":156,"assertions":5,"failures":0}
```

 {"id":"bv-w7t.7","content_hash":"1653a954f42fdc1a66f48b3e38424bfc0cce3e29894cc107ca2203ea58e3c10c","title":"Implement size-based algorithm selection","description":"## Problem

Different graph sizes need different strategies:
- Small graphs (<100 nodes): Run everything, it's fast
- Medium graphs (100-500): Run with timeouts
- Large graphs (500-2000): Skip expensive algorithms or use approximations
- XL graphs (>2000): Definitely skip betweenness, use sampling

## Solution

Implement tiered algorithm selection:

```go
type AnalysisConfig struct {
    ComputeBetweenness    bool
    BetweennessTimeout    time.Duration
    ComputePageRank       bool
    PageRankTimeout       time.Duration
    ComputeHITS           bool
    HITSTimeout           time.Duration
    ComputeCycles         bool
    CyclesTimeout         time.Duration
    MaxCyclesToStore      int
}

func DefaultConfigForSize(nodeCount, edgeCount int) AnalysisConfig {
    density := float64(edgeCount) / float64(nodeCount*nodeCount)
    
    switch {
    case nodeCount < 100:
        return AnalysisConfig{
            ComputeBetweenness: true,
            BetweennessTimeout: 2 * time.Second,
            ComputePageRank:    true,
            ComputeHITS:        true,
            ComputeCycles:      true,
            MaxCyclesToStore:   1000,
        }
    case nodeCount < 500:
        return AnalysisConfig{
            ComputeBetweenness: true,
            BetweennessTimeout: 500 * time.Millisecond,
            ComputePageRank:    true,
            ComputeHITS:        true,
            ComputeCycles:      true,
            MaxCyclesToStore:   100,
        }
    case nodeCount < 2000:
        return AnalysisConfig{
            ComputeBetweenness: density < 0.01, // Only if sparse
            BetweennessTimeout: 200 * time.Millisecond,
            ComputePageRank:    true,
            ComputeHITS:        true,
            ComputeCycles:      true,
            MaxCyclesToStore:   50,
        }
    default: // XL
        return AnalysisConfig{
            ComputeBetweenness: false, // Too expensive
            ComputePageRank:    true,
            ComputeHITS:        density < 0.001,
            ComputeCycles:      false, // Too risky
            MaxCyclesToStore:   10,
        }
    }
}
```

## TUI Indication

Show when metrics are skipped:
```
┌─ INSIGHTS ──────────────────────────────┐
│ Betweenness: [Skipped - graph too large]│
│ PageRank: 0.45 ████████░░               │
│ ...                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Density considered for sparse vs dense

 {"id":"bv-w7t.8","content_hash":"1a54743a1d5b278960125257467a8681afb28b2932a9bd1e9fdd7b4c659ce4cb","title":"Implement sampling-based betweenness approximation for large graphs","description":"## Problem

Exact betweenness centrality is O(V*E) and takes seconds for large graphs. For graphs with >500 nodes, we either skip it entirely or need an approximation.

## Solution

Implement sampling-based approximation (Brandes' approximation):

Instead of computing shortest paths from ALL nodes, sample k nodes and extrapolate:

```go
func ApproxBetweenness(g graph.Graph, sampleSize int) map[int64]float64 {
    nodes := collectNodes(g)
    n := len(nodes)
    
    if sampleSize >= n {
        return network.Betweenness(g)  // Exact for small graphs
    }
    
    // Sample k random pivot nodes
    pivots := randomSample(nodes, sampleSize)
    
    // Compute betweenness contribution from pivots only
    partialBC := make(map[int64]float64)
    for _, pivot := range pivots {
        singleSourceBC(g, pivot, partialBC)
    }
    
    // Scale up: BC_approx = BC_partial * (n / k)
    scale := float64(n) / float64(sampleSize)
    for id := range partialBC {
        partialBC[id] *= scale
    }
    
    return partialBC
}
```

## Error Bounds

With k samples, approximation error is O(1/√k):
- k=50: ~14% error
- k=100: ~10% error
- k=200: ~7% error

For ranking purposes (which node is most central), this is usually sufficient.

## Configuration

```go
type BetweennessConfig struct {
    Mode       string  // "exact", "approximate", "skip"
    SampleSize int     // For approximate mode
    Timeout    time.Duration
}

// For 1000 nodes:
// - Exact: ~1.3s
// - Approx (k=100): ~130ms (10x faster)
```

- [ ] Benchmark shows 10x+ speedup

- Paper: "Approximating Betweenness Centrality" (Bader et al., 2007)

 {"id":"bv-w7t.9","content_hash":"6a03090133b1a68fbcc5c02a27f02877505ef694c242f26e674ba49e1722a40a","title":"Add analysis result caching","description":"## Problem

When data hasn't changed, we recompute all metrics unnecessarily. This is wasteful, especially for expensive metrics like betweenness.

## Solution

Cache analysis results keyed by data hash:

```go
type AnalysisCache struct {
    mu        sync.RWMutex
    dataHash  string      // Hash of issue IDs + deps
    stats     *GraphStats
    computedAt time.Time
    ttl       time.Duration
}

func (c *AnalysisCache) Get(issues []model.Issue) (*GraphStats, bool) {
    c.mu.RLock()
    defer c.mu.RUnlock()
    
    hash := computeDataHash(issues)
    if hash == c.dataHash && time.Since(c.computedAt) < c.ttl {
        return c.stats, true
    }
    return nil, false
}

func (c *AnalysisCache) Set(issues []model.Issue, stats *GraphStats) {
    c.mu.Lock()
    defer c.mu.Unlock()
    
    c.dataHash = computeDataHash(issues)
    c.stats = stats
    c.computedAt = time.Now()
}

func computeDataHash(issues []model.Issue) string {
    h := sha256.New()
    for _, issue := range issues {
        h.Write([]byte(issue.ID))
        h.Write([]byte(issue.ContentHash))
        for _, dep := range issue.Dependencies {
            h.Write([]byte(dep.DependsOnID))
        }
    }
    return hex.EncodeToString(h.Sum(nil))
}
```

## Cache Strategy

1. **In-memory cache**: Keep last computation
2. **Hash-based invalidation**: Recompute only if data changed
3. **TTL**: Expire after 5 minutes (configurable)
4. **Partial invalidation**: If only Phase 1 metrics needed, skip Phase 2 recompute

## Integration with Refresh

When live refresh triggers:
1. Compute data hash of new issues
2. If hash matches cache, skip recomputation
3. If hash differs, recompute (async for Phase 2)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Cache stores last analysis result
- [ ] Hash-based invalidation works correctly
- [ ] Cache hit skips expensive computation
- [ ] TTL expires old cache entries
- [ ] Works with live refresh system
- [ ] Memory usage bounded

## Example
```go
func TestRenderSparkline(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        name  string
        val   float64
        width int
        want  string // or wantLen int
    }{
        {"zero value", 0.0, 10, "          "},
        {"full value", 1.0, 10, "██████████"},
        {"half value", 0.5, 10, "█████     "},
        {"clamped negative", -0.5, 10, "          "},
    }
    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            got := RenderSparkline(tt.val, tt.width)
            if len(got) != tt.width {
                t.Errorf("width = %d, got %d", tt.width, len(got))
            }
        })
    }
}
